ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'tea beverage' class; no specific entry for packaged less-sugar Chinese tea. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific code for packaged reduced-sugar Chinese tea. Closest parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for packaged less-sugar Chinese tea beverage.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve bottle or can of packaged Chinese tea (less sugar) in Singapore is approximately 250 ml, providing about 60 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Chinese Tea (Less Sugar, Packaged) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chinese tea (less sugar, packaged), cr_id D09020020. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this approved primary source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sugar-sweetened tea beverages — analogous estimate based on sucrose GI and beverage dilution; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged less-sugar Chinese tea in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on the GI of sucrose (GI=65) diluted in a tea matrix at 6g sugar/100ml, consistent with published GI values for lightly sweetened tea beverages (GI range 45–55) in Atkinson et al. 2008. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 15.0) / 100 = 7.8 for a 250g serving providing 15g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (6g/100ml) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention, despite very low sodium (6mg/100ml).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid regular consumption of this sugar-sweetened packaged tea and choose plain unsweetened Chinese tea instead, as added sugar in liquid form independently elevates blood pressure risk regardless of low sodium content (AHA 2018).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 6g sugar/100ml and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar raises postprandial blood glucose rapidly with no fibre buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this packaged less-sugar Chinese tea as it is a sugar-sweetened beverage delivering 15g of rapidly absorbed sugar per 250ml serving with no dietary fibre, causing a sharp postprandial glucose spike; plain unsweetened Chinese tea is the preferred alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased cardiovascular risk through triglyceride elevation, insulin resistance, and inflammatory pathways, even at moderate sugar levels.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit intake of this sugar-sweetened packaged tea, as habitual SSB consumption is independently associated with elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk; switching to unsweetened Chinese tea preserves polyphenol benefits without the sugar burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar with zero anti-inflammatory factors (no omega-3, no meaningful polyphenols in processed form, no whole-food fibre) negates any potential tea-derived benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should choose plain unsweetened Chinese tea to obtain polyphenol benefits without the pro-inflammatory effect of added sugar present in this packaged variant.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese Tea (Less Sugar, Packaged, Ready-to-Drink) is a commercially produced, ready-to-drink beverage widely available in Singapore, sold in bottles and cans typically of 250–500 ml. It is brewed from Chinese tea leaves (commonly oolong, green, or chrysanthemum tea base) and sweetened with a reduced quantity of sugar compared to standard sweetened tea drinks, resulting in approximately 6g of sugar per 100ml. The beverage is clear to amber in colour, mildly sweet with subtle tea astringency, and is consumed chilled or at room temperature. It is a popular everyday drink in Singapore hawker centres, convenience stores, and vending machines. Nutritionally, it provides 24 kcal per 100ml, with all energy derived from carbohydrates (6g/100ml, entirely as sugar), zero protein, zero fat, and zero dietary fibre. Sodium is very low at 6mg/100ml. Despite the 'less sugar' label, it remains classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under Singapore's Healthier Choice Symbol framework and HPB dietary guidelines. Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to rapid postprandial glucose elevation from liquid sugar with no fibre buffering. For hypertension, the added sugar independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention. For cardiovascular disease, habitual SSB consumption is associated with elevated triglycerides and inflammatory markers. Plain unsweetened Chinese tea is the recommended alternative for all these conditions, as it retains polyphenol antioxidants without the sugar burden.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D09020020), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches. GI is an analogous estimate documented with peer-reviewed basis.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero by HPB source — this is a tea-based beverage with no protein-containing ingredients. fat_g is confirmed zero by HPB source — this is a carbohydrate-only beverage with no fat-containing ingredients. saturated_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and iron_mg are confirmed zero per HPB source. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero as this is a filtered liquid beverage. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profiles for this packaged beverage are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for D09020020 as of 2026-04-16. GI estimated at 52 (low) using analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) based on sucrose GI diluted in tea matrix at 6g sugar/100ml; no direct GI measurement available in University of Sydney GI Database for this specific product. Glycemic load per 250g serving = (52 × 15.0) / 100 = 7.8. This beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under HPB Singapore guidelines despite the 'less sugar' label. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology code exists for packaged less-sugar Chinese tea.
